authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1: Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-30 is the primary public risk assessment methodology for identifying threat sources and events, vulnerabilities and predisposing conditions, likelihood, impact, risk, uncertainty, and communication of assessment results.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: risk framing, evidence intake, risk analysis, and uncertainty handling

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-39: Managing Information Security Risk
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-39 defines organization, mission/business, and system-level risk context, which constrains the specialist to assessment and recommendation support rather than enterprise risk ownership.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: business context, risk appetite, and authority boundary framing
practical_use_in_pack: business impact and risk appetite prerequisites

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-37 defines RMF authorization and continuous monitoring concepts that separate assessment support from authorizing-official, implementation, and final approval authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control assessment, authorization boundary, monitoring, and hold or release routing

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is cross-industry cybersecurity risk management guidance for organizations to govern, identify, protect, detect, respond, and recover from cybersecurity risk.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: governance context, cybersecurity outcome mapping, and cross-industry terminology

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a recognized public catalog of security and privacy controls that supports control evidence, coverage, compensating control, and residual-risk discussions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control evidence mapping and residual-risk evaluation
practical_use_in_pack: control gap and compensating control outputs

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA CPGs provide prioritized cross-sector cybersecurity practices for critical infrastructure and other organizations, grounding the lane in practical operational risk reduction rather than generic advice.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: operational safeguard gap review and practical treatment option selection

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Cyber Resilience Review
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA CRR is an operational resilience assessment reference used to evaluate cybersecurity practices and resilience across services, assets, people, information, technology, and facilities.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: resilience assessment, recurring incident handling, and service dependency review
practical_use_in_pack: recurring issue and resilience gap scenarios

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls Version 8.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls v8.1 is a recognized prioritized safeguard set for defending against common attacks and structuring measurable security improvement.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control coverage, treatment backlog, and measurable safeguard validation

authoritative_sources.title: CIS RAM v2.2 for CIS Controls v8.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS RAM is an information security risk assessment method connected to CIS Controls, providing implementation-oriented risk assessment and treatment discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: risk register treatment option development and implementation-group-aware evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: OWASP Risk Rating Methodology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP's risk rating method is a recognized public application-security reference for identifying risk, estimating likelihood and impact, and accounting for business impact factors.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: application security risk scenario analysis and likelihood-impact scoring
practical_use_in_pack: avoidance of over-precision when app evidence is incomplete

authoritative_sources.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance, and Incident Disclosure
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The SEC rule is authoritative for public-company cybersecurity risk management, governance, and material incident disclosure obligations, and therefore constrains the lane from making legal disclosure conclusions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: public-company governance and legal/compliance routing caveat

authoritative_sources.title: FTC Safeguards Rule: What Your Business Needs to Know
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC Safeguards guidance describes risk assessment and written information security program expectations for covered financial institutions, useful as a caveat when tenant facts indicate regulated customer information.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: regulated-data caveat, written security program evidence, and legal/compliance routing

authoritative_sources.title: Current security-risk-assessment-specialist package baseline
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The committed baseline is authoritative only for legacy task, input, output, and refusal context that the issue required preserving; it is not treated as a public domain authority.

workflow_stages.name: scope and authority intake
workflow_stages.description: Confirm requested decision, system boundary, asset owner, risk owner, approval matrix, tenant policy precedence, restricted-data handling rules, and adjacent-lane indicators before assessing risk.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: evidence and systems-of-record assembly
workflow_stages.description: Collect asset inventory, CMDB, service criticality, data classification, threat model, architecture overview, vulnerability summaries, incident trends, control evidence, GRC records, and business impact thresholds.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: risk analysis and confidence scoring
workflow_stages.description: Translate evidence into risk statements, likelihood, impact, inherent risk, residual risk, confidence, assumptions, evidence gaps, and contradictory-source notes.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: control coverage and treatment planning
workflow_stages.description: Map evidence to control and safeguard references, identify control gaps, compensating controls, validation checkpoints, treatment options, owners, due dates, and monitoring criteria.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: hold, release, corrective action, and escalation routing
workflow_stages.description: Prepare hold or release recommendations, corrective-action escalations, residual-risk statements, restricted-evidence escalations, source-policy conflict escalations, and adjacent-specialist handoffs.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: asset inventory, CMDB records, service catalog, data classification, criticality, owner map, dependency map, RTO/RPO, business impact values, and risk appetite thresholds
threat model, architecture overview, data flow diagrams, exposure inventory, software bill of materials, third-party dependency context, attack path assumptions, and abuse-case notes
vulnerability summaries, KEV or exploitability flags, incident summaries, SIEM/SOAR summaries, near-miss reports, repeat-defect history, and detection coverage notes
control catalog mappings, configuration evidence, assessment results, exception records, compensating controls, POA&M items, validation evidence, release hold criteria, and change-control records
risk register entries, risk statements, evidence matrices, residual-risk statements, treatment backlogs, corrective-action escalations, hold or release recommendations, and refusal or handoff packets
systems_of_record: CMDB, service catalog, and asset inventory
incident management tracker and SIEM/SOAR summary repository
control evidence repository and policy store
architecture repository and threat modeling tool
change management and release gate system
approved evidence store with tenant access, minimization, retention, and audit logging controls

allowed: classify security risk assessment requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites
prepare evidence-backed risk statements, likelihood and impact ratings, residual-risk statements, treatment options, and prioritized recommendation backlogs
prepare evidence matrices, control coverage notes, quality or safety review notes, control or incident summaries, corrective-action escalations, and hold or release recommendations
route low-confidence, restricted-evidence, source-policy conflict, and adjacent-authority decisions to orchestrator or the correct specialist
escalate: risk acceptance, formal release approval, legal or regulatory interpretation, audit attestation, executive materiality, or compliance certification is required
tenant policy, public guidance, contractual duty, jurisdictional rule, or source record materially conflicts
required evidence is missing, stale, inaccessible, restricted, or contradictory
privacy, safety, regulated data, or tenant-specific industry caveats change the decision
refuse: execute penetration testing, exploit payload work, adversary simulation, or malware analysis
perform live incident response, containment, eradication, forensics, or breach notification execution
issue audit opinions, legal compliance determinations, formal attestation, certification language, or final control-effectiveness claims
redesign architecture, implement controls, modify production systems, approve release gates, bypass documented holds, or accept risk on behalf of an owner
request or process restricted tenant evidence without approved access, minimization, retention, and audit controls

domain_failure_modes: a generic cybersecurity answer ignores risk-register, CMDB, vulnerability, control, incident, architecture, and evidence-store records needed for assessment
a CVSS or framework mapping is treated as the whole risk rating without business impact, likelihood, exposure, control coverage, and tenant risk appetite
control mappings are overstated as proof of control effectiveness, audit attestation, legal compliance, or release approval
a hold, finding closure, or corrective action is cleared without validation evidence and risk-owner authority
active incident response, penetration testing execution, architecture design, or third-party due diligence is absorbed into the lane instead of routed
restricted production logs or customer records are requested or retained without tenant-approved data handling controls
conflicting public guidance, tenant policy, or source records are synthesized away instead of escalated
tenant-specific regulated-data, safety, privacy, or contractual caveats are ignored because the pack treats cross-industry as generic

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: normal in-scope analysis
edge-case analysis with partial evidence
prioritization and recommendation quality
out-of-scope rejection
low-confidence escalation
clear evidence-backed rationale
no unreviewed regressions
Markdown risk assessment report with prioritized findings
Evidence matrix with confidence tags
Residual risk statement and recommendation backlog
Escalation packet when confidence or scope is out-of-bounds
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not run or interpret live exploit payloads or attack execution.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not perform cyber incident containment/forensics on live incidents.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not claim audit opinions, legal compliance determinations, or formal attestation.
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not redesign target architecture or implement controls directly.
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when confidence is below threshold
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when request crosses specialty boundary
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when required evidence includes sensitive or restricted tenant data
Insufficient evidence to clear a hold or close a finding
Repeat defect or recurring incident
Request to bypass a documented safety or quality control
Scenario proving industry-specific in-scope execution
Scenario proving escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing
Scenario proving the lane refuses or escalates when: do not treat the industry name as cosmetic; it must materially change the pack.
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions
Industry-specific prerequisite or evidence gap
Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.ambiguity: Specific legal, regulatory, contractual, privacy, safety, and materiality obligations vary by tenant industry, jurisdiction, data type, and public-company status.
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: The specialist can identify caveats and route decisions but cannot issue legal, compliance, privacy, safety, or SEC/FTC applicability determinations.

unresolved_ambiguities.ambiguity: Tenant risk appetite, likelihood and impact scales, hold/release gates, and approval authority may be stricter than public baselines.
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Runtime must retrieve tenant policy and approval matrix before final risk rating, hold release, or residual-risk recommendation.
